explanation:
The natural language requirement states that: (1) 'The local instance of matrix M for DU_i must be updated with a frequency 1 so that any DU can update its navigation data every second' and (2) 'Each DM transmits its local matrix instance to the other DMs within 1 time unit.' 

Assuming 'matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2' (etc.) represents the event that the matrix update is sent from one DM to another DM, and that 'dm1_performs_update' represents a local update, then: 

To formalize the property 'each DM transmits its local matrix instance to the other DMs within 1 time unit (every second)', for each recipient DM (say DM2), G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 -> X F_{<=1} matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2). However, since LTL with just G, F, X, U does not support explicit time-bounded temporal operators (e.g., F_{<=1}), we approximate this timing requirement as 'after every local update, the update is sent, and the receiver eventually receives it.'

Additionally, to enforce 'the update of M by DM1 occurs infinitely often (with frequency 1)' we encode it as GF(dm1_performs_update) (meaning it occurs infinitely often, ideally every unit time).

The translation also implies that after each update is sent, its receipt is eventual, i.e., G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 -> F(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2)), and similarly for the other DMs, to encode the transmission to all DMs. 

So, the full LTL is: 
GF(dm1_performs_update) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 -> F(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2)) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 -> F(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3)) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 -> F(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4)) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 -> F(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5))

explanation_dict.natural_language: DM1 performs an update infinitely often (every unit time).
Whenever a matrix update is sent by DM1 to DM2, DM1 will eventually receive an update from DM2.
Whenever a matrix update is sent by DM1 to DM3, DM1 will eventually receive an update from DM3.
Whenever a matrix update is sent by DM1 to DM4, DM1 will eventually receive an update from DM4.
Whenever a matrix update is sent by DM1 to DM5, DM1 will eventually receive an update from DM5.

output_LTL: GF(dm1_performs_update) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 -> F(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2)) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 -> F(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3)) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 -> F(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4)) & G(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 -> F(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5))

explanation:
The sentence says: 'The local instance of matrix M for DU_i must be updated with a frequency 1 so that any DU can update its navigation data every second. This implies that each DM transmits its local matrix instance to the other DMs within 1 time unit.' 

First, 'every DU can update its navigation data every second' requires that each DM receives an up-to-date matrix from the others at least once per time unit. Mapping this to LTL using atomic propositions, and assuming 'matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2' stands for DM1 sending a matrix to DM2, and similarly for others, we want to capture that each message is sent infinitely often ('with frequency 1', i.e., at least once per time unit).

In LTL, the pattern 'happens infinitely often' is represented as GF (globally, finally...), so for each DMi, the matrix update to DMj must occur infinitely often: GF(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2) and similarly for all other pairs.

But 'within 1 time unit' is hard to capture strictly in LTL (we would need a timed extension). The best we can do is require that after each clock tick, each DM receives the update from every other DM. Assume 'clock_occurred' signals this time unit passing, and 'matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2' is the reception event.

So, for every clock tick, globally, it holds that for each DM (e.g., DM1), after a clock occurs, eventually (before the next clock) DM1 receives an update from every other DM. In LTL, for DM1, for example:

G(clock_occurred -> (F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & F matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)) 

This formula means: globally, whenever a clock tick occurs, DM1 will in the future receive a matrix update from DM2, DM3, DM4 and DM5, at least once (until the next tick, which is outside the expressive power of LTL, but F is as close as we can get).

Assume this applies for every DM. If we generalize, the structure for one DM is as above.

explanation_dict.natural_language: DM1 must receive a matrix update from DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5 after every clock tick
After each time unit (clock tick), DM1 will eventually receive the matrix updates from each other DM
This condition holds globally (always, for every clock tick)

explanation: The requirement implies that each DM (Distributed Module) must transmit (send) its local matrix to every other DM within one time unit, with the effect that every DM can update their navigation data every second (suggesting at least once every cycle, i.e., infinitely often). In LTL, 'infinitely often' is expressed by GF. The property 'within 1 time unit' in LTL with Next (X) means the event must happen in the next state. If we let matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 represent a local instance being sent from DM1 to DM2, then for every pair (DMi, DMj), we want: 'Globally, in every state, it is always the case that a matrix update from DMi to DMj will happen in the next state'. Since we have several DMs (indices 1-5), we have to express this for every pair. For an abstract representation, we can use the proposition matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 for each pair as an example. The general pattern is: G(F(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2)), etc., but with the time constraint, it becomes: G(X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2)), etc. Finally, to cover 'any DU can update its navigation data every second', meaning every DM can receive the update periodically, we have G(F(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2)), etc.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Each DM transmits its local matrix instance to the other DMs within 1 time unit.
Each DM receives a matrix update from every other DM every second (infinitely often).

output_LTL: G(X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2)) & G(X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3)) & G(X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4)) & G(X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)) & GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2) & GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3) & GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4) & GF(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)

explanation:
The sentence expresses two requirements: (1) that the local matrix M in any DU is updated at least once every time unit (frequency 1), so navigation data can update every second, and (2) that every DM transmits its local matrix instance to others within 1 time unit. In LTL, the first requirement means that for all time (globally), for each DU, GF (infinitely often) matrix update is sent to each other DU, and each receives it within 1 time unit. The simplest encoding is to ensure that for each pair (e.g., DM1 to DM2), 'infinitely often, if a clock tick occurs, then within the next time unit a matrix update is sent from DM1 to DM2, and DM2 receives it in the next state.' So, for each DM, G(clock_occurred -> X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2))). This is repeated for all DMs. To capture 'within 1 time unit', we use the 'next' (X) operator. In summary, for every clock tick, within one time unit, each DM must send its matrix update to every other DM, and the recipient must receive it in the next time step.

Consolidating for DM1 and its communication with others as an example:  G(clock_occurred -> (X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2)) & X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3)) & X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4)) & X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 & X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5)))).  

For all DMs, this pattern should be generalized. If only DM1 is considered, the formula is as above.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Frequency 1 update for matrix M
Each DM transmits to others within 1 time unit after a clock tick
explanation_dict.LTL: G(clock_occurred -> (X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2)) & X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3)) & X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4)) & X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 & X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5))))

output_LTL: G(clock_occurred -> (X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2)) & X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3)) & X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4)) & X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 & X(matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5))))

explanation: The requirement says that (1) every local instance of matrix M for DU_i must be updated once per second (frequency 1), so every DU can update its navigation data every second; and (2) to achieve this, each DM must send its local matrix to the other DMs within 1 time unit. Focusing on (2) as the enforceable temporal property, for each pair of DMs (e.g., DM1 and DM2), whenever a clock event occurs (representing the passage of 1 second), DM1 must send a matrix update to DM2 within 1 time unit (next step). This generalizes pairwise. In LTL for a single pair, this is: G(clock_occurred -> X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2)). For all DMs, this would be repeated for each pair. For the update property: Each DU_i sends its local matrix infinitely often (with period 1), which is GF(dui_sends_local_matrix_to_services) for all DU_i. Using your provided atomic propositions, and focusing on DM1 to other DMs as example, the main enforceable temporal property is: G(clock_occurred -> X(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)).

explanation_dict.natural_language: Each time unit, DM1 must send a matrix update to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5.
The update happens once per frequency cycle (per tick of the clock)

explanation: The requirement is that every local instance (for every DM) of matrix M is updated with frequency 1 (i.e., once per time unit), so every DM can update its navigation data every second. Operationally, this means that each DM must transmit its local matrix to every other DM within 1 time unit (globally, for all time units). Let 'matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2' be the atomic proposition that represents DM1 sending an update to DM2; likewise for DM3, DM4, and DM5. We want to say: globally, for each target DM (e.g. DM2), it is always the case that within every time unit, a matrix update is sent from DM1 to DM2 (and similarly for other DMs). This can be expressed as: Globally, it is always true that, in the next step (or within one time unit), a matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 occurs. Since LTL does not have explicit counters, we use GF for 'infinitely often' or use 'G F p' to state that p occurs in every future (i.e., at least once in every time window). However, for exact periods, in classic LTL, we cannot say 'exactly every second', but we can say 'infinitely often'. To approximate, let's use G F(matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2)... etc., for all DMs except DM1 (assuming DM1 sends to DM2, DM3, DM4, DM5).

explanation_dict.natural_language: DM1 sends a matrix update to DM2 every time unit (infinitely often)
DM1 sends a matrix update to DM3 every time unit (infinitely often)
DM1 sends a matrix update to DM4 every time unit (infinitely often)
DM1 sends a matrix update to DM5 every time unit (infinitely often)

explanation: To capture the requirement that each local instance of matrix M must be updated at frequency 1 (every time unit), so that any DU can update its navigation data every second, and that each DM transmits its local matrix instance to all other DMs within 1 time unit, we must ensure that globally, for every time unit (let's take 'clock_occurred' as the atomic proposition for a time unit), each DM sends its update to every other DM, and each DM receives an update within the next time unit. This property is most simply interpreted as: for every occurrence of 'clock_occurred', within the next time unit, every other DM receives a matrix update message from the sender. For DM1 as an example, we can write: G(clock_occurred -> X (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)). This guarantees that after every clock occurrence, within the next step, DM1 must send matrix updates to all other DMs.

explanation: The sentence describes a periodic requirement (every second) that the local matrix instance at each DM (Distributed Module) is updated and this update is transmitted to all other DMs within 1 time unit. This periodic update can be mapped onto LTL as 'Globally, DM1 performs an update implies that for all other DMs DM2...DM5, a matrix update is sent from DM1 to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5 in the next time instant.' To express that each DM performs this update infinitely often (i.e., with frequency 1), we use GF(dm1_performs_update), meaning DM1 will perform an update infinitely often (i.e., at least once in every window of the time frame). Furthermore, whenever an update is performed, the sending of the update to each DM must occur in the next step (within 1 time unit). This is captured with G(dm1_performs_update -> (X matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & X matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & X matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & X matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)). The full specification is GF(dm1_performs_update) & G(dm1_performs_update -> (X matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & X matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & X matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & X matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)).

explanation: The sentence describes that every DM (Distributed Module) must transmit its local matrix to all other DMs every time unit (with frequency 1), allowing any DM to update its navigation data every second. In LTL, this means that each DM must, in every time unit, send its local matrix to the other DMs, so that for every DM pair, globally (G), in every state, it is required (->), that in the next time step (X), the matrix update is sent. If we abstract with an atomic proposition like 'clock_occurred' for the time tick and 'matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2' (and so on for others) as the transmission, then for every clock tick, in the next step, a matrix update transmission must occur from DM1 to DM2, DM1 to DM3, etc. The LTL formula will use a conjunction for all DMs. Thus, for DM1, the LTL can be: G(clock_occurred -> (X matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & X matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & X matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & X matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5)). This ensures that, every time unit, all required updates are sent out.

explanation: The sentence describes a requirement that each Data Manager (DM) must update its local matrix with frequency 1 (every time unit), so that every Data Unit (DU) can update its navigation data every second. This operation is realized by ensuring that each DM sends its local matrix to the other DMs within 1 time unit. We express this periodic update and communication as follows: For every time unit (globally, G), if a clock tick occurs (clock_occurred), then for each pair of DMs, a matrix update is sent from DM1 to DM2 (and similarly to DM3, DM4, DM5) and is eventually received by the respective DM within the next time unit (expressed with X F ...). This ensures the update and transmission happen within one time unit. The periodicity is modeled with the G (globally) operator, and communication with X F (within next time unit). To encode that this is valid for every DM pair, given the provided atomic propositions, we write one formula for each pair (using the given propositions), resulting in a conjunction of such LTL formulas for all relevant DMs. We group together the explanation for one such pair (DM1 to DM2) as an example; similar formulas are written for the other pairings.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Update of the local matrix must occur every time unit.
Each DM must send its matrix update to each other DM within 1 time unit.
This property must hold for every DM.
explanation_dict.LTL: conjunction over all relevant DM pairs
